evidence: Docstring asks 'What is the sofa constant?', the precise question of the moving sofa problem referenced via the Wikipedia header link.
Docstring states Gerver's sofa attains the sofa constant, 'conjectured by [Ge92] and claimed by [Ba24]' — the moving sofa problem's central claim.
Docstring states 'Gerver's sofa is the unique sofa that attains the sofa constant', the open uniqueness form of the moving sofa problem.

The Noether Problem asks whether every fixed field of a rational function field is a rational extension; the file's header references the Wikipedia article Rational_variety where Noether's problem is treated.
The statement 'pi is normal in base 10' is an instance of the open normality question referenced via the Wikipedia (Normal number) header link.
The conjecture asserts IsNormalInBase 10 π for the constant pi, per the Wikipedia (Pi) header reference.
Docstring '**Oppermann's Conjecture**: for every integer x >= 2 there is a prime between x(x-1) and x^2 and between x^2 and x(x+1)' matches the referenced Wikipedia statement exactly.
The conjecture bounds gaps between consecutive primes; the header cites Ferreira's 'Real exponential sums over primes and prime gaps'.
First half of Oppermann's conjecture: a prime between x(x-1) and x^2, per the docstring.
Second half of Oppermann's conjecture: a prime between x^2 and x(x+1), per the docstring.
Docstring: 'Ferreira proved that Oppermann's conjecture is true for sufficiently large x'.
The conjecture bounds the pebbling number of a graph product; the header references the Wikipedia article Graph_pebbling.
The statement bounds PebblingNumber (G □ H), the Cartesian product of graphs, by the product of the factors' pebbling numbers.
Docstring 'There are infinitely many prime Pell numbers', per the Wikipedia Pell_number#Primes_and_squares header reference.
Docstring '**Infinitely many perfect numbers conjecture.** Are there infinitely many perfect numbers?' with Wikipedia Perfect_number reference.
Docstring asks whether there are infinitely many even perfect numbers.
Docstring: 'This is equivalent to asking whether there are infinitely many Mersenne primes' via the Euclid-Euler theorem.
Docstring '**Odd Perfect Number Conjecture.** ... all perfect numbers are even', i.e. no odd perfect number exists, per Wikipedia Perfect_number#Odd_perfect_numbers.
The statement asserts every Perfect n is Even, a claim about perfect numbers.
Docstring cites Ochem-Rao (2012): an odd perfect number must exceed 10^1500 and have at least 101 prime factors.
Docstring cites Euler's theorem on odd perfect numbers: any odd perfect number has the form p^a * m^2 with p prime, p = a = 1 mod 4.
